data.title: Renewi's CEO Steps Down
data.description:
By Najat Kantouar

Renewi said Chief Executive Otto de Bont is stepping down from his role and that successor will be announced in due course.

The London-listed waste management company said Friday that under de Bont's leadership, the company experienced significant growth and expanded its customer base.

De Bont has served as CEO since 2019.

Write to Najat Kantouar at najat.kantouar@wsj.com

data.title: Berenberg: Allianz Shares No Longer Undervalued, Price Target Up
data.description:
Berenberg raised its price target on Allianz to 419 euros from 407 euros, while keeping the financial services stock at buy.

"Based on a comparison against its historical metrics, we no longer consider Allianz to be undervalued," analysts said Thursday. "In addition, a comparison of the change in Allianz's P/E ratio with the change in Allianz's hybrid bond spread also suggests that the equity market still values Allianz more fairly than the bond market."

The research firm also said there is "some seasonality" to the German stock as its five-year heatmap showed consistently positive first- and fourth-quarter performances, with the summer season being less consistent.

data.title: Australia Could Ease U.S. Beef Import Rules Amid Tariff Tensions
data.description:
By Stuart Condie

SYDNEY--Australia could ease rules on U.S. beef imports amid tensions over U.S. tariffs including on steel and aluminum.

Australia allows the import of beef from cattle raised and slaughtered in the U.S. However, beef from U.S.-slaughtered cattle raised in Mexico and Canada isn't allowed into a country with strict biosecurity rules designed to protect local industry and animals.

Australia's Prime Minister Anthony Albanese on Friday said his government could consider modifying the rules. White House officials called out Australia's policy on beef imports when justifying the imposition of tariffs earlier this year.

"If things can be sorted out in a way that protects our biosecurity, of course we don't just say 'no,'" Albanese said in a radio interview.

"We don't want imports in here for the sake of it. Our first priority is biosecurity and there'll be no compromise on that."

As a major producer, Australia hasn't imported any U.S. beef since 2005. Beef was Australia's seventh-largest export by value in its last full fiscal year, behind mined commodities including iron ore and travel-related services.

Write to Stuart Condie at stuart.condie@wsj.com

data.title: Market Chatter: UBS Hires Two Tech Investment Bankers From Bank of America
data.description:
UBS hired David Larsen and Rob Michlovich from Bank of America to strengthen its technology investment banking team in New York, Reuters reported Thursday, citing an internal memo.

Larsen will reportedly join in September to focus on application software, while Michlovich starts in July as managing director, covering industrial and supply chain software.

The pair has more than 30 years of combined experience advising on major mergers and acquisitions and capital markets transactions, Reuters reported.

UBS and Bank of America did not immediately respond to MT Newswires' request for comment.

Disclaimer

Market Chatter news is derived from conversations with market professionals globally. This information is believed to be from reliable sources but may include rumor and speculation. Accuracy is not guaranteed.

data.title: Polyvinyl chloride (PVC) prices continue to gain in Turkey
data.description: Despite weaker ethylene feedstock values, PVC prices marched higher in Turkey.An industry source in Turkey, while requesting to remain unidentified, informed a Polymerupdate team member, " This week, the Turkey PVC spot market experienced a price rise, bolstered by the most attractive offers reported and a broad increase in suggested offer prices. Price increase was additionally bolstered by the optimistic feedstock ethylene, EDC, and VCM prices. Simultaneously, market players observed a decrease in trading activity as Eid al-Adha neared, with purchasers taking a cautious approach and refraining from buying in bulk quantities. Previously, the market had been facing weak demand, as purchasers were hesitant to agree to new shipments. The source added ,

data.title: Low density polyethylene (LDPE) prices remain steady in Europe
data.description: This week, LDPE prices rolled over in the European region.An industry source in Europe informed a Polymerupdate team member, "European low-density polyethylene (LDPE) spot prices stayed generally stable this week, indicating balanced-to-ample supply fundamentals and limited trading activity. Despite small variations, the market has mostly remained stable, supported by indications of ample supply and a lack of urgency from purchasers. The European polyethylene (PE) market is experiencing significant downward pricing pressure because of a consistent stream of competitively priced imports from the U.S., the Middle East, and Asia. This steady import presence has weakened sellers' capacity to increase prices, particularly in the spot market, where buyers display little interest beyond urgent needs. Overall supply continues to be plentiful, with market players expressing no worries regarding material accessibility. Various sources indicated that converters and distributors possess diverse sourcing alternatives, further strengthening market length. In the meantime, demand has remained mostly unchanged. Although spot inquiries are scarce, contract market activity demonstrates a relatively steady offtake�especially from the agricultural film and flexible packaging sectors. With less than six weeks left in the ongoing 90-day EU�U.S. tariff suspension, numerous traders are avoiding additional imports of HDPE, LDPE, and LLDPE from the U.S. Due to standard transatlantic shipping durations and persistent port congestion at various European hubs, the opportunity to safely receive new shipments before July has pretty much ended. However, U.S.-sourced material�especially HDPE�continues to appear economically appealing on paper, with significant arbitrage opportunities still available. Should a risk appetite be present among opportunistic sellers relying on a formal EU�U.S. trade agreement or another tariff pause extension, some additional volumes might still be organized. Nonetheless, these agreements now entail increased financial and logistical risk. The source added, "Market sentiment has similarly been influenced by changing factors in U.S. trade policy. Even with legal ambiguity, signals from the White House imply that tariffs may be enforced in some capacity unless a trade agreement is formally inked. Consequently, participants in the European PE market remain wary, with numerous buyers sticking to a necessity-driven approach, steering clear of forward purchases and speculative imports. Given that the fundamental demand continues to be weak and no significant enhancement is anticipated shortly, the European LDPE market could persist in an extended downturn. Numerous value chains�especially in consumer products, construction materials, and industrial packaging�are exhibiting indications of structural deceleration. In this situation, both purchasers and vendors seem to be taking a "wait-and-see" stance, focusing on liquidity and inventory control rather than strategies driven by volume. In the spot markets, LDPE prices were assessed at the Euro 1115-1125/mt FD Northwest Europe mark, unchanged week on week.In the contract markets, General Purpose LDPE grade prices were assessed at the Euro 1910-1915/mt FD NWE Germany and FD NWE Italy levels, both stable from the previous week. LDPE General Purpose grade prices were assessed at the Euro 1910-1915/mt FD NWE France levels, flat from last week. Meanwhile, LDPE grade prices were assessed at the GBP 1610-1615/mt FD NWE UK levels, a week on week gain of GBP (+10/mt).FCA Antwerp LDPE General Purpose film prices were assessed at the Euro 1100-1120/mt levels, left unchanged from the previous week.Ethylene spot prices on Thursday were assessed at the Euro 685-695/mt FD North West Europe levels, up Euro (+15/mt) week on week.European Ethylene contract price for June 2025 settled at the Euro 1135/MT FD North West Europe levels. This price represents a rollover from its May 2025 settlement levels.

data.title: Russia Cuts Interest Rate by 100bps
data.description:
The Bank of Russia cut its key interest rate by 100 basis points to 20.00% on June 6, citing continued easing in inflationary pressures, including core inflation.

Inflation slowed to 9.8% year-on-year as of June 2, with underlying indicators ranging from 5.5–7.5%.

A drop in consumer price growth, especially for non-food items, reflected the effect of tight monetary policy and a stronger ruble.

Still, inflation for food and services remains elevated, and inflation expectations—particularly among households—stay high.

While the labor market remains tight, signs of easing labor shortages and wage moderation are emerging.

Credit activity is subdued, and households continue to save.

The Bank warned that inflation risks still outweigh disinflationary ones and highlighted the potential impact of fiscal changes or external shocks.

The Bank signaled it would maintain tight monetary conditions for an extended period to bring inflation back to the 4% target by 2026.

data.title: Polyvinyl chloride (PVC) prices quote flat in Europe
data.description: This week, PVC prices remained constant in the European region.An industry source in Europe informed a Polymerupdate team member, "The European polyvinyl chloride (PVC) market saw limited activity, with market players initiating early contract discussions for June. Concurrently, multiple traders chose to halt acquisitions of new materials from Asia due to increasing freight worries. After the June ethylene contract price (CP) settlement, focus turned to PVC contract negotiations. Discussions stayed in their initial phases. Producers reaffirmed their plan to enhance margins regardless of ethylene price fluctuations, indicating that current profit margins are not sustainable. If European producers uphold stable pricing in the upcoming months, arbitrage possibilities from Asia are expected to continue. Nonetheless, rising freight costs from Asia are introducing unpredictability to import plans. Multiple traders have paused new orders for now, particularly for deliveries anticipated in August, a time usually marked by a seasonal decline in PVC demand in Europe. Although certain sources mentioned a slight increase in demand during the week, the majority of market participants indicated that there was no significant rise in seasonal demand from the construction industry, which typically fuels PVC usage. On the supply side, availability throughout Europe continued to be plentiful. Freight rates from Asia are not only increasing but also skyrocketing, fuelled by scarce container supply, rerouting caused by geopolitical conflicts, and port congestion at crucial transit locations. This trend is expected to continue exerting pressure on import economics for the near future. The market continues to exercise caution, with the price trend for July anticipated to rely significantly on the recovery of construction demand and freight movements. In the spot markets, FD North West Europe PVC prices were assessed at the Euro 875-885/mt levels, left unchanged from the previous week. In the contract markets, PVC Suspension grade prices were assessed at the Euro 955-960/mt FD NWE Germany and FD NWE France levels, both steady week on week. PVC Suspension grade prices were assessed at the Euro 965-970/mt FD NWE Italy levels, stable from last week. Meanwhile, PVC prices were assessed at the GBP 800-805/mt FD NWE UK levels, unchanged week on week.The source added, "The PVC market in Europe is still traversing May contract discussions, with downward pricing pressure dominating in all regions. Even with the general lacklustre sentiment, producers indicated that demand has been stronger than expected, providing a somewhat more positive perspective than some buyers. Nonetheless, the overall attitude is cautious. Buyers are demanding larger price cuts, yet numerous local manufacturers in Europe seem optimistic about recovering some profit in May, particularly following months of reduced profitability. Demand from the construction industry remains weak, with uses such as window frames and piping still encountering challenges. Moreover, the surge in imports of finished goods�especially in the vinyl flooring category�is putting pressure on demand for certain domestic manufacturers. A market participant characterized the vinyl flooring market as "saturated," pointing out that consumer adoption is sluggish. The demand for emulsion PVC (e-PVC) continues to be sluggish. The European automotive industry, a major user of e-PVC, remains underperforming. While export levels have provided some comfort to producers, persistent trade conflicts have clouded the forecast. Specifically, e-PVC shipments to the United States are facing difficulties because of a 10% tariff imposed on imports from the European Union. Last Friday, the situation intensified when former President Trump suggested a universal 50% tariff on all European exports to the U.S., starting June 1. Although the move might eventually act as leverage in trade talks, the immediate unpredictability is hindering preparations for numerous European manufacturers. Meanwhile, feedstock EDC prices were assessed at the USD 160-170/mt FOB North West Europe levels while VCM prices were assessed at the USD 500-510/mt FOB North West Europe levels, both up USD (+5/mt) from the previous week.Ethylene spot prices on Thursday were assessed at the Euro 685-695/mt FD North West Europe levels, a gain of Euro (+15/mt) from last week.European Ethylene contract price for June 2025 settled at the Euro 1135/MT FD North West Europe levels. This price represents a rollover from its May 2025 settlement levels.

data.title: Acrylonitrile butadiene styrene (ABS) spot prices plunge in Europe
data.description: This week, ABS spot prices tumbled, while contract prices quoted flat in the European region.An industry source in Europe informed a Polymerupdate team member, "Market conditions in the European acrylonitrile butadiene styrene (ABS) market remained lacklustre in early June, as demand continued to be weak because of ongoing underperformance in major downstream industries. The automotive sector, a key end-use market for ABS, experienced a further decline in sentiment and future expectations. Continuing worries about possible tariffs, especially concerning electric vehicle imports from Asia, have reduced investor confidence and disrupted procurement plans. A market participant candidly labeled the automotive scenario as a catastrophe, emphasizing the seriousness of the downturn in activity. The appliance and electronics industries, which are other key factors influencing ABS usage, also demonstrated weak performance, thus restricting the rebound in demand. Numerous converters are still functioning at lower rates, maintaining minimal stock levels due to uncertainty regarding order quantities and price forecasts. The source added, "Provisional measures in the Korean and UNV1 antidumping duty investigations are anticipated to be implemented this month, while the market waits for the same to be announced officially. These actions have garnered some interest, particularly in light of the increasing examination of imports from Asia. Nevertheless, the mood among market participants is careful, with minimal anticipation for any significant immediate effects. Past antidumping measures in other polymer sectors�like PET and PP�have mostly not succeeded in driving substantial demand recoveries or pricing power. In the present environment, market players are prioritizing inventory balance over speculative purchases. The European ABS supply chain is said to be stable, with no significant production problems, yet abundant availability still impacts sentiment negatively. Feedstock prices (comprising butadiene, acrylonitrile, and styrene) have stayed comparatively stable in the past few weeks, alleviating cost-push pressures and aiding in price stagnation. Compounding the tepid situation are logistical challenges in specific regions of Europe, such as port congestion and container shortages, although their direct effect on the ABS trade flow has been minimal up to this point. Export activity stays low, with weak global demand and currency fluctuations restricting arbitrage possibilities. In conclusion, the European ABS market still encounters challenges due to low demand, economic uncertainty, and limited confidence in the success of trade actions. As players attentively watch the forthcoming antidumping measures for potential changes in import patterns or sentiment, anticipations remain rooted in the overarching context of oversupply and sluggish end-user engagement. In the spot markets, FD Northwest Europe general purpose and natural grade ABS prices were assessed at the Euro 1695-1705/mt levels, sharply decline by Euro (-50/mt) week on week.In the contract markets, general purpose and natural grade ABS prices were assessed at the Euro 1900-1905/mt FD NWE Germany and FD NWE Italy levels, both unchanged from the previous week. ABS prices were assessed at the Euro 1900-1905/mt FD NWE France levels, steady from last week. Meanwhile, ABS prices were assessed at the GBP 1600-1605/mt FD NWE UK levels, a marginal rise of GBP (+5/mt) from last week.Upstream SM spot prices on Thursday were assessed at the USD 1090-1100/mt FOB Rotterdam levels, a steep decline of USD (-110/mt) from last week.Butadiene spot prices were assessed at the USD 815-820/mt FOB Rotterdam levels, a week on week fall of USD (-10/mt). Meanwhile, ACN prices were assessed at the USD 1445-1455/mt CIF MED levels, a drop of USD (-10/mt) week on week.European Styrene contract price for June 2025 has been settled at the Euro 1409/mt CIF North West Europe. This price represents a sharp fall of Euro 47/mt from its May 2025 settlement levels.European Butadiene contract price for June 2025 settled at the Euro 920/MT FD North West Europe levels. This price represents a sharp drop of Euro (-50/mt) from its May 2025 settlement levels.

data.title: Otsuka's kidney disease drug shows over 50% reduction in protein in urine
data.description:
Otsuka's experimental drug for a type of kidney disease showed over 50% reduction in protein in patients' urine in a late-stage trial, the Japan-based company said on Friday.

Patients treated with the therapy, sibeprenlimab, achieved a 51.2% reduction in proteinuria at nine months of treatment when compared to placebo.

The company is seeking approval for the therapy, a monoclonal antibody, to treat Immunoglobulin A Nephropathy (IgAN), also known as Berger's disease, which causes abnormal protein buildup in the kidneys and could eventually lead to the organ's failure.

It was the largest IgAN trial to date, which consisted of about 510 adult patients the company said.

The U.S. health regulator is set to decide on the marketing application of the therapy by November 28.

Shares of rival Vera Therapeutics fell over 31% in premarket trading.

Vera's experimental drug, atacicept, reduced protein levels in patients' urine by 46%, compared with a 7% reduction with a placebo, meeting the main goal of the 428-patient late-stage study, the company said on Monday.

data.title: Trump and Musk Fallout Is Box Office. But This Matters More for Markets. — Barrons.com
data.description:
The fraught discussions playing out between President Donald Trump and one of the world's most powerful figures could shape his presidency and the direction of the market. His playground fight with Elon Musk isn't helping, either.

OK, that might seem a little flippant. But Trump's call with Chinese leader Xi Jinping on Thursday was boosting markets before the blowup with Tesla CEO Musk grabbed everyone's attention. In the long-term, the U.S.-China negotiations are still where investors should be focused.

Trump and Musk's fallout isn't inconsequential. Wiping more than $150 billion off Tesla's valuation and calling into question the U.S. government's relationship with its dominant space launch provider in Musk's SpaceX shook the market. But tempers seem to be cooling, with a call between the two to be held Friday, according to Politico.

Will that call be more productive than the one between Trump and Xi Jinping? While the leaders of the world's two largest economies talking is definitely a positive, there appeared to be little progress made on any agreement to permanently lower tariffs.

There's plenty of incentive to do a deal, with tariffs causing economic pain. China's central bank injected around $139 billion of liquidity into markets on Friday, looking to offset bank-deposit withdrawals in the face of lower interest rates designed to cushion the trade-war blow. Meanwhile, U.S. economic data has been soft this week and on the eve of the May jobs report, BlackRock CEO Larry Fink warned of "very elevated inflation" if tariffs are implemented.

Whether Trump and Musk make up is of pressing interest for Tesla investors and fans of social-media drama. But whether he reaches a deal with Xi Jinping matters for everyone.

  • Adam Clark

***

Trump-Musk Breakup Mesmerizes Internet With Insults, Threats

Political observers got the drama they had anticipated in the once-close relationship between President Trump and Tesla CEO and government cost-slasher Elon Musk. The world watched mesmerized as the two men hurled insults, barbs, and threats at each other, and their bromance rapidly dissolved.

  • Musk has been trashing Trump's signature legislative effort, which GOP lawmakers call the "One Big Beautiful Bill" containing Trump's tax and spending priorities. From the Oval Office, Trump said Musk's criticism was "very disappointing." He later suggested spending cuts could come by canceling Musk's government contracts.
  • That was like tossing gasoline on the fire. In a rapid-fire string of social media posts, Musk said he would decommission SpaceX's Dragon rocket, the vehicle used to transport astronauts to the space station, he agreed with someone on X that Trump should be impeached, and he said tariffs would cause a recession.
  • Taunting the president, Musk said "Make my day," in response to Trump's post threatening to cancel his federal contracts. Trump said Musk's presence as a senior advisor in the administration had worn thin and he asked the billionaire to leave. The president told reporters Musk's criticisms could be "Trump derangement syndrome."
  • Musk appeared to be insulted by Trump's Oval Office remarks, posting that the president wouldn't have been elected without his support and that the Democrats would control the House. "Such ingratitude," he added. Trump claims Musk was upset that the tax bill eliminates the electric vehicle tax credit.

What's Next: Musk's feud with Trump erased $150 billion from Tesla's market value, one of its worst days ever. But Musk's SpaceX, a private company, also has much to lose. SpaceX is the world's dominant space launch provider, and NASA accounts for about $15 billion of total awards to the commercial space company.

  • Anita Hamilton and Al Root

***

Trump, Xi Call on Tariffs Leaves Plenty of Problems

President Trump and Chinese leader Xi Jinping held talks on Thursday but investors shouldn't get their hopes up. The two spoke by phone to discuss rare earth minerals and trade, defusing a tit-for-tat that had put into question the fragile truce reached last month.

  • Having the world's two largest economies on talking terms, with both leaders inviting the other for a visit and sending top officials to meet their counterparts again on trade, was a near-term positive.
  • The call was aimed at breaking an impasse sparked by differing interpretations of what the two sides agreed on in the May talks in Geneva. It reduces the risk that the levies could return to the trade-paralyzing levels seen before the May agreement.
  • The way the two governments presented Thursday's discussion, however, added to skepticism any real progress was made. "The U.S. side should acknowledge the progress already made, and remove the negative measures taken against China," said a readout on the call by Beijing. Trump, meanwhile, described the call and his relationship with Xi positively.

What's Next: Geopolitical strategists saw little that meaningfully reduces the uncertainty hanging over businesses. Investors and companies now looking forward to the next meeting between high-level U.S. officials and their counterparts may want to lower their expectations.

  • Reshma Kapadia

***

Circle's Soaring IPO Paves the Way for More Crypto Offerings

Circle Internet Group, the leader in the world of stablecoins, closed its first day of trading up nearly 170%, in a sign that crypto mania is back. Circle CFO Jeremy Fox-Geen told Barron's after the stock began trading that it was "still the early days for our mission."

  • Circle listed 34 million shares on the New York Stock Exchange under the ticker symbol CRCL. It opened at $69 a share and quickly soared to $103.75, or 235% above its initial public offering price of $31 a share. Shares closed at $83.23, a 168% gain.
  • At $83.23, Circle has a market value of about $18.4 billion. (The value would increase to $21.4 billion including options, warrants, and shares that could be purchased by underwriters.) The company and its investors raised nearly $1.1 billion from the stock sale.
  • Circle is the issuer of USDC, a cryptocurrency pegged to the U.S. dollar whose price hovers around $1, hence its classification as a stablecoin. Stablecoins like USDC and Tether's rival USDT token are considered better for payments and settlements, since their prices don't fluctuate as wildly as Bitcoin, Ethereum, and XRP.
  • Circle's USDC trails Tether's USDT, though. According to data from CoinMarketCap, USDT has a market value of $153.8 billion compared with $61.5 billion for USDC. Third Bridge analyst Jacob Zuller thinks Circle will look to diversify its revenue and focus more on payments.

What's Next: Zuller said more stablecoin regulation would help Circle, which is based in New York City, catch up to Tether, which moved its headquarters to El Salvador, where Bitcoin is a legal currency. He predicts Circle's stablecoin market share could grow to 40%, from 28%.

  • Paul R. La Monica and Janet H. Cho

***

Lululemon Cuts Outlook as Tariffs Weigh on Consumers

Athletic apparel maker Lululemon Athletica beat expectations for the first quarter but slashed its outlook, demonstrating the tricky road companies are trying to navigate in the world of tariffs, supply chains, and consumer worries about household spending.

  • For the quarter ending in May, Lululemon reported earnings of $2.60 a share and a 7% gain in revenue to $2.37 billion. But same-store sales were a stumbling block, rising just 1% compared with expectations for a rise of 4.1%.
  • Lululemon's weak profit guidance was affected by higher tariff-related costs and more markdowns. It now sees full-year earnings from $14.58 to $14.78, down from its prior guidance of $14.95 to $15.15. Second-quarter earnings are projected to be $2.85 to $2.90, below expectations.
  • It plans to offset tariffs, including by taking "strategic price increases" and more efficiency in sourcing, but these will provide more relief in the second half of the year than the first half. Full-year revenue guidance remains at $11.15 billion to $11.3 billion.
  • Even though revenue seems more stable than earnings, investors are still concerned about lackluster sales growth in the Americas, Lululemon's biggest market. Same-store sales in the region fell 2% from a year ago, and executives said store visits were soft in the first quarter compared with the fourth quarter.

What's Next: Consumers in the U.S. remain cautious right now, according to CEO Calvin McDonald, speaking with analysts on a call. "They are being very intentional about their buying decisions."

  • Sabrina Escobar

***

Housing Inventory Available for Sale Back at 2019 Levels There are now more houses available for sale in the U.S. than at any time since late 2019, but they are staying on the market longer. Home buyers in the south and west will find it easier to house-hunt this year, while northeast and Midwest buyers won't be quite as lucky.

  • The number of homes for sale in May rose 31.5% to more than one million, reaching a level not seen since December 2019 just before the pandemic, Realtor.com data show. (News Corp, which owns Barron's, also owns Realtor.com operator Move.)
  • Homes are also staying on the market for a median of 51 days, six more than a year ago. About one in five homes listed for sale had a price cut, the highest percentage since at least July 2016, Realtor.com said.
  • Inventory has returned to prepandemic levels in southern and western metro areas, but not in the northeast or Midwest. "Not every housing market is equally well-supplied," Realtor.com chief economist Danielle Hale said. Areas where the most new homes were built during and after the pandemic are seeing listings mount.
  • Of the 50 largest metropolitan areas Realtor.com tracked: Denver; Austin, Tex.; Seattle; San Antonio; and Dallas saw the greatest rise in inventory compared with prepandemic levels. Areas where inventory declined the most are Hartford, Conn.; Chicago; Virginia Beach, Va.; Providence R.I.; and Cleveland.

What's Next: The 30-year fixed-rate mortgage averaged 6.85% on Thursday, according to Freddie Mac. It was the first rate decline in five weeks. Samir Dedhia, CEO of One Real Mortgage, called it "a window of opportunity for both buyers and those looking to refinance."

  • Shaina Mishkin and Janet H. Cho

***

Do you remember this week's news? Take our quiz below to test your knowledge. Tell us how you did in an email to thebarronsdaily@barrons.com.

1. The U.S. dollar index, which tracks the greenback against a basket of six global currencies, is down 8.4% over the first five months of the year, according to Dow Jones Market Data. It is trading near the lowest levels since when?

a. Summer of 2022 b. Winter of 2020 c. Summer of 2014 d. Fall of 2000

2. Bristol Myers Squibb has jumped into the drug industry's most intense new rivalry in a bid to unseat Merck's megablockbuster cancer drug Keytruda. For Bristol Myers, that means an $11 billion deal to jointly develop a cancer drug with which of the following?

a. Moderna b. BioNTech c. Teva Pharmaceutical d. None of the above

3. Elon Musk stepped up his attacks on the tax and spending bill, telling his 220 million X followers it should be killed and a new one drafted that doesn't "massively grow" the deficit. The nonpartisan Congressional Budget Office projects it adds how much to the deficit over 10 years?

a. $4.4 trillion b. $3.4 trillion c. $2.4 trillion d. $1.4 trillion

4. Illinois lawmakers approved a new per-bet tax on sportsbooks that weighed heavily on DraftKings and Flutter Entertainment-owned FanDuel, which together have how much of the online betting market share in the state?

a. 75% b. 50% c. 25% d. None of the above

5. A 20-year deal by Meta Platforms to buy nuclear power is a sign Big Tech's artificial intelligence boom is just beginning. Which of the following made the deal with Meta?

a. Nuscale Power b. Oklo c. PG&E d. Constellation Energy

Answers: 1( a); 2( b); 3( c); 4( a); 5( d)

  • Barron's staff

***

  • Newsletter edited by Liz Moyer, Rupert Steiner

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Diesel spot diffs at three-week high; July refiner sales in focus
data.description:
Asia's middle distillates' market activity centered around July spot refiner sales, against a backdrop of more unplanned supply outages in Japan, while spot premiums hit three-week highs.

July refiner sales for diesel was the market's focus this week, with at least three major northeast Asian refiners kickstarting discussions - though deals done were mostly still in discounted territory linked to free on board (FOB) Singapore quotes.

At least three 300,000-barrel and two 750,000-barrel cargoes of 10ppm sulphur gasoil and two 500ppm sulphur gasoil cargoes of 300,000 barrels each were sold via tenders or private discussions.

Buying interest was mixed across the regions, though some cautiousness remained on slowing demand in a few regions amid monsoon season.

For jet fuel, activity was slightly more muted though the emergence of some east-west arbitrage flows did perk up the market slightly and offset weakness from the closed Asian arbitrage window to the U.S west coast.

Refining margins (GO10SGCKMc1) barely moved week on week, closing the week slightly below $16 a barrel.

On the trading window, spot deals were mostly at premiums of 40-60 cents per barrel in the week, with cash differentials (GO10-SIN-DIF) closing 58% higher week on week and at a three week high.

Regrade (JETREG10SGMc1) held its ground at discounts of around $1.20 a barrel.

SINGAPORE CASH DEALS

- Two gasoil deals, no jet fuel deal

INVENTORIES

- Gasoil stocks independently held in the Amsterdam-Rotterdam-Antwerp (ARA) refining and storage hub, which include diesel, rose nearly 1% to 2 million tonnes, data from Dutch consultancy Insights Global showed on Thursday.

REFINERY NEWS

- Japanese oil refiner Idemitsu Kosan shut the 155,000 barrels-per-day No.3 crude distillation unit (CDU) at its Yokkaichi refinery in central Japan on May 19 for scheduled maintenance, a company spokesperson said on Friday.

- Irving Oil said on Thursday it plans to invest $100 million to upgrade a key crude oil processing unit at its 320,000 barrel-per-day Saint John facility in New Brunswick, Canada's largest refinery.

NEWS

- Growth in China's exports likely slowed in May despite a lowering of U.S. tariffs on Chinese goods, as the fallout from the still-unresolved trade war and uncertainties in Sino-U.S. ties weighed on shipments.

data.title: The SENSEX Index Closes 0.90% Higher
data.description:
In Mumbai, the SENSEX Index gained 735 points or 0.90 percent on Friday.

Gains were led by Bajaj Finance (4.90%), Indusind Bank (4.30%) and Axis Bank (3.15%).

Biggest losers were Tata Steel (-0.97%), Bharti Airtel (-0.46%) and Sun Pharmaceuticals (-0.24%).

(a)        Indemnity and other dealing arrangements

Irrevocable commitments and letters of intent should not be included.  If there are no such agreements, arrangements or understandings, state "none"

(b)        Agreements, arrangements or understandings relating to options or derivatives

If there are no such agreements, arrangements or understandings, state "none"

data.title: Emerging market FX set to end week near record highs
data.description:
Key points:
  • Emerging FX and stocks flat, set for weekly gains
  • Russian rouble down ahead of rate decision
  • Indian stocks rise after larger-than-expected rate cut

Emerging market assets were set to close the week higher on Friday, supported by a weaker dollar, as investors navigated U.S. President Donald Trump's trade manoeuvres that have dictated market sentiment throughout the week.

The Russian rouble fell 2.6% against the U.S. dollar ahead of the central bank's decision on its key interest rate, with expectations in a Reuters poll pointing to a decision to hold.

Increasingly, however, analysts have said the sharp slowdown of the economy and a decelerating inflation rate could lead the central bank to surprise the market with a cut at the upcoming meeting, triggering a weakening of the rouble.

The MSCI index of emerging market currencies (.MIEM00000CUS) held steady near an all-time high hit in the previous session. The index, up 0.5% so far this week, is on track for its seventh weekly advance in eight.

A parallel index of emerging market equities CBOE:EFS dipped 0.1%, but hovered near an eight-month peak and was up more than 2.1% for the week.

The dollar index edged higher ahead of Friday's jobs data that could dictate the next move for the currency.

Erratic U.S. trade policy has weighed on the dollar, supporting capital inflows into emerging markets as global investors look at international arenas to diversify their portfolio in the hope of better returns.

"There's a good chance that it's just the start of a bigger trend. For the first time in many years, there's a little bit of interest in emerging markets and that will lead to some flows, more likely to come from the U.S.," said Rob Brewis, fund manager at Aubrey Capital Management.

Markets assessed Thursday's much-anticipated hour-long telephone call between Trump and Chinese leader Xi Jinping after weeks of trade tensions. The dialogue gave no clear indication of a breakthrough, although the leaders left open the possibility of further talks.

In Poland, the main stock index and the local currency the zloty were on pace for weekly losses, down 3.9% and 1.1% respectively, on fiscal concerns following eurosceptic Karol Nawrocki's presidential win.

The central bank on Thursday said it will not announce the interest rate path because of the economic uncertainty. The bank had left its main interest rate unchanged at 5.25% earlier this week.

On the day, Poland's banking index WIG banki fell 3.5% after its parliament speaker proposed the introduction of a windfall tax on lenders' excess profits to the coalition agreement.

Meanwhile, Hungary's forint fell 0.6% after data showed industrial output fell more than expected in April. The currency was largely unchanged for the week.

Investors looked ahead to Fitch's review of Hungary's credit rating. The Budapest stocks ticked down 0.1%, taking its weekly gains to 0.8%.

In the Czech Republic, the main stock index held steady after first-quarter output growth exceeded 2% year-on-year. For the week, the index was up 0.4%.

The Romanian leu on Friday was up 0.1%, after the economy expanded by 0.3% year-on-year in the first quarter, surpassing an initial flash estimate. Over the week, it has gained 0.33%.

Elsewhere, Indian shares advanced about 1% after the Reserve Bank of India cut its key repo rate by a larger-than-expected 50 basis points and lowered the reserve ratio for banks as the central bank shifts its focus to boosting growth.

For TOP NEWS across emerging markets
